name: DMAIC (Six Sigma)
year_developed: 1986 at Motorola; formalized as DMAIC methodology late 1980s-early 1990s; major revision: integration with Lean as Lean Six Sigma early 2000s; PMBOK integration ongoing through 6th and 7th editions
progenitor: Bill Smith (credited as 'father' of Six Sigma) and Mikel J. Harry at Motorola; championed by Motorola CEO Bob Galvin; popularized globally by Jack Welch at General Electric in the 1990s; statistical foundations trace to Walter Shewhart (1920s process control) and Carl Friedrich Gauss (bell curve)
driving_insight: The central insight is that process variation is the root cause of defects, and defects can be reduced to near-zero (3.4 per million opportunities) only by rigorously measuring, analyzing, and controlling variation. The 'Measure' phase — quantifying the gap between current and target performance — is the critical differentiator from less rigorous problem-solving approaches. Without measurement, you don't know whether a problem is real, how big it is, or whether your solution actually fixed it. The methodology bridges the gap between 'we think there is a problem' and 'we have statistically verified that the problem is reduced.' Six Sigma transforms quality from an art into a science by making every decision data-driven.
decision_style: algorithmic and data-driven — decisions are made based on statistical evidence, not intuition or authority. Hypothesis testing, regression analysis, control charts, and designed experiments replace opinion. The methodology is hierarchical in governance (Master Black Belts mentor Black Belts who lead Green Belts) but the decisions themselves are empiric — data, not rank, determines the answer. The structured tollgate review process adds a deliberative layer where Belt-level practitioners present evidence to project sponsors for go/no-go decisions.
problem_class_targeted: well-defined, measurable problems in existing processes with stable boundaries; problems where outcomes can be quantified (defect rate, cycle time, cost) and a baseline can be established; most effective on repetitive, high-volume processes with clear customer requirements; designed for existing process improvement, not new process design (DMADV covers new design); well-suited for static or slowly changing process environments; less suited for ill-defined, exploratory, or rapidly changing problems where baselines cannot be established
ordered_phases: Five sequential phases with tollgate reviews between each: (1) DEFINE — clarify the problem, project goals, scope, and customer requirements; outputs include project charter, voice of the customer (VOC), SIPOC diagram (Suppliers-Inputs-Process-Outputs-Customers), and CTQ (Critical to Quality) tree; (2) MEASURE — quantify current process performance; collect baseline data, validate measurement system (Gage R&R), establish process capability (Cp, Cpk), and quantify the gap between current and target; (3) ANALYZE — identify and statistically verify root causes of defects/variation; use tools including 5 Whys, fishbone (Ishikawa) diagrams, Pareto charts, hypothesis testing, regression analysis, and ANOVA; (4) IMPROVE — develop, test, and implement solutions addressing verified root causes; use brainstorming, design of experiments (DOE), mistake-proofing (poka-yoke), pilot testing, and FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis); (5) CONTROL — sustain the gains and prevent regression; implement control charts, standard operating procedures (SOPs), statistical process control (SPC), monitoring plans, and response plans for out-of-control conditions. Each phase ends with a formal tollgate review before proceeding.
feedback_loops: Feedback loops are explicit and designed: (1) The Control phase creates a permanent monitoring feedback loop — SPC charts continuously feed process performance data back to operators, triggering corrective action when the process drifts; (2) The Analyze-Improve loop is implicit but standard — if root causes cannot be statistically verified, the team loops back to Measure for better data or to Analyze with different hypotheses; (3) Tollgate reviews create a human feedback loop — if a phase's deliverables don't meet criteria, the team reworks that phase; (4) The final Control-to-Define loop is organizational — if the process degrades despite controls, a new DMAIC project may be chartered; (5) Post-project benefits realization tracking feeds back into the organizational Six Sigma program metrics. All loops are explicit, documented, and part of the formal methodology.
gates: Tollgate reviews are formal approval gates between every phase, typically conducted by a project Champion or Sponsor with the Belt leading the project. Define-to-Measure gate: project charter approved, scope confirmed, VOC and CTQs documented; blocks if problem is poorly defined or not aligned with business priorities. Measure-to-Analyze gate: baseline data collected, measurement system validated (Gage R&R <30%), process capability established; blocks if measurement system is inadequate or baseline is unreliable. Analyze-to-Improve gate: root causes statistically verified (p<0.05 typically), not just hypothesized; blocks if root causes are speculative — requires return to data collection or hypothesis generation. Improve-to-Control gate: pilot results demonstrate statistically significant improvement, FMEA shows acceptable residual risk, implementation plan resourced; blocks if improvement is not statistically significant or pilot reveals new risks. Control-to-Closure gate: control plan in place, SPC charts stable, process owner signed off, financial benefits validated by finance; blocks if controls are inadequate or benefits cannot be sustained. When a gate blocks, the team returns to the prior phase or earlier — this is a formal, documented decision with criteria.
exit_criteria: A DMAIC project is 'done' when: (1) The Control phase tollgate is passed — control charts show stable process at the improved level for a defined period (typically 30+ data points); (2) The process capability (Cpk) meets the target (typically Cpk >= 1.33 for existing processes, >= 1.50 for new); (3) Financial benefits are validated by the finance department (not self-reported by the team); (4) The process owner has formally accepted handoff of the control plan; (5) All project documentation is archived in the organizational knowledge base. Partial completion states: the project may be closed early if (a) the root cause is found to be outside the team's control (escalation), (b) the business case no longer justifies continued effort, or (c) a 'quick win' achieves sufficient improvement before full DMAIC completion. Projects that fail to pass a tollgate are not 'done' — they are either reworked or formally terminated.
decomposition_method: DMAIC decomposes hierarchically but differently from pure task decomposition: (1) In Define, the project scope is broken into CTQs (Critical to Quality characteristics) — the customer requirements decomposed into measurable specifications; (2) SIPOC decomposes the process from end-to-end into Suppliers, Inputs, Process steps, Outputs, and Customers; (3) In Measure and Analyze, the process is further decomposed into sub-processes and process steps, each measured for defects and variation; (4) Root cause analysis decomposes the problem into causal factors (fishbone diagram categories: Man, Machine, Method, Material, Measurement, Mother Nature/Environment); (5) The process decomposition is value-driven (focused on what creates defects) and domain-driven (structured by process steps). Decomposition stops at the level where a root cause can be statistically verified and a countermeasure can be assigned to a single owner. This is fundamentally a measurement-driven decomposition — you decompose to the level where variation can be reliably measured and controlled.
granularity_guidance: Granularity is driven by statistical measurability: (1) Decompose until you reach a process step where defect data can be reliably collected with a valid measurement system (Gage R&R <30%); (2) A root cause is 'leaf-level' when it can be attributed to a specific, controllable factor that can be experimentally verified; (3) Work packages (Improve phase actions) follow standard project management sizing — typically 40-80 hours; (4) The process map granularity is determined by the level at which variation becomes assignable — if two consecutive steps have the same defect profile, they may be analyzed together; (5) Practical rule: decompose until each leaf node has a single measurable output (Y) and a manageable set of potential inputs (X's, typically 3-7 per Y). The recursion stops when further decomposition would not increase the ability to measure or control variation — the 'measurement resolution limit.'
boundary_rules: Clear boundary rules are enforced: (1) Project charter explicitly defines in-scope and out-of-scope — what process, what location, what product line; (2) SIPOC defines the end-to-end process boundary — starts with supplier inputs, ends with customer outputs; (3) MECE is enforced — each defect is counted in exactly one category (Pareto categories must be mutually exclusive); (4) The Y = f(X) transfer function ensures clean boundaries — each output (Y) is traced to specific inputs (X's) with verified causal relationships; (5) Overlap between DMAIC projects is actively managed by the Six Sigma program office — two projects cannot claim savings from the same defect reduction; (6) Process ownership boundaries are clarified at the Control phase handoff — the process owner takes responsibility for the bounded process with defined KPIs; (7) When boundaries are unclear (e.g., a defect crosses process boundaries), a cross-functional team is chartered with representatives from all boundary-spanning areas.
risk_assessment_mechanism: Risk assessment is structured and tool-driven: (1) FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis) — systematic identification of potential failure modes for each process step, scored on Severity (1-10), Occurrence (1-10), and Detection (1-10), multiplied to produce a Risk Priority Number (RPN); actions required for RPN > threshold (typically 100-200); (2) Control charts provide ongoing risk monitoring — out-of-control signals trigger investigation; (3) Measurement System Analysis (Gage R&R) assesses the risk of bad data leading to wrong conclusions; (4) Design of Experiments (DOE) quantifies the effect size and interaction effects of multiple variables, reducing the risk of implementing ineffective solutions; (5) Pilot testing (Improve phase) contains the blast radius of failed solutions; (6) Risk assessment is primarily pattern-matching (FMEA) and probabilistic (statistical process control), with human expert judgment for severity and occurrence ratings; (7) Business risk is assessed through the project charter's business case and validated by finance. The methodology is checklist-driven but statistically rigorous — risk decisions are supported by data, not intuition.
unknown_handling: When encountering something unknown: (1) In Measure — if the measurement system cannot reliably detect a defect, the team halts and improves the measurement system first (Gage R&R); you cannot improve what you cannot measure; (2) In Analyze — if root causes cannot be verified, generate new hypotheses and collect additional data; do not proceed to Improve with unverified causes (halt-and-investigate); (3) In Improve — if a proposed solution's effect cannot be demonstrated in a pilot, do not proceed to full-scale implementation (default-safe); (4) Unknown process variation is treated as 'common cause' until proven 'special cause' — this is a conservative default that prevents tampering with stable processes; (5) For novel defect types not seen before: escalate to Master Black Belt for methodology guidance; (6) The methodology defaults conservative — do not implement a solution until its effectiveness is statistically demonstrated. The system is fundamentally a variance-reduction engine — unknown variation is the enemy to be eliminated through measurement and analysis.
default_stance_on_unknown: RESTRICTIVE — the methodology explicitly blocks progression to the next phase until the current phase's unknowns are resolved. The Measure phase gate requires a validated measurement system — unknown measurement error blocks progress. The Analyze phase requires statistically verified root causes — unknown causality blocks progress. The Improve phase requires demonstrated improvement in a pilot — unknown effectiveness blocks full-scale rollout. This is the essence of DMAIC: do not act until you have data, do not implement until you have proof. The methodology's rigor comes from this restrictive stance — it is designed to prevent premature, intuition-driven solutions.
safety_guardrails: Specific safety mechanisms: (1) Tollgate reviews — each phase requires formal approval from a Champion/Sponsor before proceeding; no phase can be skipped; (2) Pilot testing — solutions are tested at small scale before full deployment, containing the blast radius of failed improvements; (3) Control charts with action limits — automatically signal when a process goes out of control, triggering investigation and containment; (4) FMEA with RPN thresholds — forces systematic consideration of failure modes before implementation; (5) Mistake-proofing (poka-yoke) — designs processes so that errors are physically impossible or immediately detectable; (6) Measurement System Analysis — validates that the measurement system itself is reliable before using its data for decisions (prevents 'garbage-in, garbage-out'); (7) Financial validation by independent finance department — prevents teams from overstating savings; (8) Process owner sign-off at Control phase — ensures operational accountability for sustained performance. These are all designed for human-executed manufacturing and transaction processes, not for autonomous agent execution — there are no I/O firewalls, sandboxing, or attack-class defenses relevant to LLM agents.
human_decision_points: Humans are deeply embedded at every phase and every tollgate. Specific human decision points: (1) Project selection and chartering — leadership selects which problems warrant a Six Sigma project (not all do); (2) Tollgate reviews — Champion/Sponsor approves progression at each of the 4 gates; (3) CTQ definition — humans determine what constitutes a 'defect' from the customer's perspective; (4) Root cause judgment — statistical tools narrow the field, but humans select the final root cause from the shortlist; (5) Solution selection — humans weigh cost, feasibility, and risk to choose among statistically valid solutions; (6) FMEA scoring — Severity, Occurrence, and Detection ratings are human expert judgments; (7) Process owner acceptance — the operational handoff is a human decision. The Belt practitioners (Green, Black, Master Black Belt) are trained and certified humans. Humans are IN the loop — they execute the analysis, make the judgments, and own the outcomes. The methodology is entirely dependent on trained human practitioners.
override_capability: Yes, at multiple levels: (1) The project Champion can halt or redirect a DMAIC project at any tollgate; (2) The Belt can escalate to the Champion if they encounter obstacles outside their authority; (3) The process owner can reject the Control plan if it is operationally infeasible; (4) The Six Sigma program office can de-charter projects that no longer align with business priorities; (5) Individual Belt practitioners can override statistical defaults (e.g., choosing a different alpha level) with justification; (6) Override is per-project (full halt) and per-phase (redirect to earlier phase). There is no per-action override in an automated execution sense because the methodology has no automated execution — every action is human-executed and therefore inherently overridable. Granularity: decisions can be challenged at the level of individual root cause hypotheses, individual solution ideas, and individual control chart rules.
fallback_when_no_human: DMAIC cannot function without trained human practitioners. If no Belt is available: (1) No new DMAIC projects can be initiated; (2) Active projects stall at the next tollgate — there is no auto-approve; (3) Control phase monitoring (SPC charts) may continue if operators are trained to respond to out-of-control signals, but methodology-driven improvement stops; (4) Existing process controls (SOPs, control charts) persist indefinitely — the process does not degrade, but it does not improve; (5) There is no timeout-and-default mechanism — the methodology blocks until a qualified human is available. In an LLM-agent context, statistical analysis could be automated, but the human judgments (CTQ definition, solution selection, process owner acceptance) have no automated surrogate. The methodology degrades to a monitoring-only state without humans — it blocks on improvement, which is tolerable (status quo maintained) but not desirable.
max_problem_size: Demonstrated on enterprise-scale manufacturing operations with thousands of processes across global supply chains — GE, Motorola, Honeywell, and others applied Six Sigma company-wide. Practical limits: (1) A single DMAIC project typically addresses one process or sub-process with a manageable number of CTQs (3-10); (2) Large problems are decomposed into multiple DMAIC projects managed as a program; (3) What breaks at scale: the tollgate process becomes bureaucratic overhead — projects take 3-6 months on average, and coordinating dozens of projects with shared resources creates portfolio management complexity; (4) Data collection costs scale linearly with process complexity — measuring every step of a complex process can be more expensive than the expected savings; (5) Theoretical limit: DMAIC is bounded by the ability to establish a stable measurement baseline — for highly unstable or one-of-a-kind processes, baseline cannot be established, so DMAIC cannot start. The largest demonstrated deployment is company-wide Six Sigma programs with thousands of concurrent projects (e.g., GE under Jack Welch).
parallel_execution: Yes, parallelism is first-class at the program/portfolio level: (1) Multiple DMAIC projects run in parallel across different processes, products, and business units; (2) Dependencies are resolved at the portfolio level — the Six Sigma program office manages resource allocation (Belt availability, data collection resources) and ensures projects do not conflict (two projects improving the same process must be coordinated); (3) Within a single DMAIC project, phases are sequential — you cannot Measure before you Define, cannot Analyze before you Measure; (4) Within the Analyze phase, multiple root cause hypotheses can be investigated in parallel; (5) Within the Improve phase, multiple solutions can be piloted in parallel on different process segments; (6) Parallelism is managed through Belt training tiers — Master Black Belts oversee multiple Black Belts who each run multiple projects. The methodology is designed for organizational-scale parallelism but enforces sequential phase progression within each project.
team_scaling_model: Linear-to-sub-linear scaling with structured role hierarchy: (1) A single DMAIC project typically involves 4-8 team members led by a Black Belt or Green Belt; (2) Scaling is achieved by training more Belts and chartering more projects — each Belt can lead 1-4 projects depending on tier; (3) Green Belts (part-time, lead 1-2 projects) scale linearly — more Green Belts = more projects; (4) Black Belts (full-time, lead 3-4 projects, mentor Green Belts) provide sub-linear scaling — each additional Black Belt supports 15-20 Green Belts; (5) Master Black Belts (program-level, train and mentor Black Belts) scale super-linearly in impact — one MBB can multiply the effectiveness of 10-20 Black Belts; (6) Negative scaling occurs when the Belt-to-project ratio exceeds training pipeline capacity — untrained practitioners applying tools incorrectly produce unreliable results and erode program credibility; (7) The recommended ratio: 1% of workforce as Black Belts, 5% as Green Belts. Team size per project is capped at ~10 to maintain effective collaboration.
cost_scaling_model: Cost scales linearly with the number of projects and practitioners: (1) Training costs: Green Belt ~$2K-5K, Black Belt ~$10K-20K, Master Black Belt ~$30K+; (2) Project execution costs: primarily the opportunity cost of team members' time (4-8 people at 10-25% time each for 3-6 months); (3) Data collection costs scale with process complexity — each additional CTQ or measurement point adds data collection effort; (4) For LLM-based DMAIC automation: cost would scale with the number of process steps analyzed, the number of hypotheses tested, and the volume of data processed; (5) The tollgate review branching factor: 4 gates, each a binary pass/fail; failed gates create rework loops, increasing total cost by ~20-40% per rework cycle; (6) Overall: costs are predictable and stable (roughly linear in problem size) because the phase structure and tollgates bound scope creep. The methodology is cost-efficient for high-volume processes where defect reduction savings (>$50K typically) justify the investment.
replanning_built_in: Replanning is formal but limited: (1) Tollgate reviews are the formal replanning triggers — if a phase's deliverables fail review, the team replans that phase; (2) The project charter can be revised at any tollgate if scope or goals need adjustment (requires Champion approval); (3) Replanning is primarily local — you rework the current or prior phase, not restart the entire project; (4) Major replanning (return to Define) occurs when the root cause is found to be outside the project scope or when the business case collapses; (5) Replanning is a first-class part of the methodology — the tollgate structure exists precisely to catch and correct before proceeding; (6) However, replanning is reactive (triggered by tollgate failure) rather than proactive — there is no built-in 're-examine assumptions' trigger for projects that are passing tollgates but heading toward failure. The methodology presumes that passing tollgates correlates with project health, which is usually but not always true.
trigger_mechanism: Adaptation is triggered by: (1) Tollgate review failure — a phase's deliverables do not meet the gate criteria (most common trigger); (2) Statistical signals — control charts detect out-of-control conditions triggering investigation and corrective action; (3) New data — baseline shifts, new defect types emerge, or previously hidden variation is uncovered; (4) Scope change — business priorities shift, making the original project goals obsolete; (5) Solution failure — pilot results show the solution did not achieve the expected improvement (Type II error in root cause analysis); (6) Customer requirement change — VOC analysis reveals new or changed CTQs. There is no automated detection — Belt practitioners and Champions must recognize triggers and act. The control chart is the only automated trigger mechanism (SPC rules detect out-of-control conditions automatically, but human interpretation is still required).
adaptation_cost: Cost of replanning is moderate and phase-dependent: (1) Define rework: low cost — re-scoping and charter revision require 1-2 weeks; (2) Measure rework: moderate cost — re-collecting baseline data or re-validating measurement systems may take 2-6 weeks; (3) Analyze rework: moderate-to-high cost — new hypotheses require new data collection and statistical testing; (4) Improve rework: high cost — a failed pilot means the solution investment is partially lost, and a new solution must be developed and tested; (5) State preservation is good — all phase deliverables are documented, so the team can resume from the last passed gate rather than starting from zero; (6) Lost work is contained to the current and immediately prior phase — you don't lose work from phases that passed their tollgates; (7) Overall: the tollgate structure bounds rework cost — it prevents the worst case of discovering a fundamental flaw only at rollout.
failure_recovery_mechanism: Recovery follows the DMAIC structure itself: (1) Detection — failures are detected through tollgate reviews, control chart signals, or post-project benefits tracking; (2) Isolation — the failed element is traced to the phase where the error originated (e.g., a wrong root cause was an Analyze-phase failure); (3) Recovery — the team returns to the failed phase, re-executes it with corrected inputs, and progresses through subsequent phases again; (4) Retry is formal — the team replans the phase with explicit corrective actions; (5) Local compensation is preferred — fix the specific error rather than restarting the project; (6) Global replan is used when the project charter itself is invalid (wrong problem, wrong scope); (7) Blast radius containment — the phase-gate structure limits the blast radius to the current and subsequent phases; errors caught at earlier gates have smaller blast radii; (8) Human escalation — if the team cannot resolve the failure, it escalates to the Champion and ultimately to the Six Sigma program office; (9) Graceful degradation — if the project is terminated, existing process controls remain in place; the process does not degrade below its pre-project baseline. The methodology's sequential phase structure is itself a failure recovery mechanism — errors are caught at gates before propagating to later phases.
output_artifacts: Each phase produces standardized artifacts: Define: project charter, SIPOC diagram, VOC summary, CTQ tree, stakeholder analysis; Measure: data collection plan, measurement system analysis (Gage R&R), process capability report (Cp, Cpk, Pp, Ppk), baseline performance metrics; Analyze: fishbone diagram, Pareto chart, hypothesis test results, regression analysis, verified root cause list; Improve: solution selection matrix, pilot test results, DOE results, FMEA (re-scored post-improvement), implementation plan, cost-benefit analysis; Control: control plan, SPC charts, SOPs, training materials, process owner sign-off, project closure report; Program-level: project portfolio dashboard, financial benefits tracking, Belt certification records, lessons learned database.
briefing_format: Tollgate review presentations are the standard briefing format — a structured PowerPoint deck (typically 20-40 slides) walking through each phase's deliverables in sequence. Key elements: (1) Project charter summary (one slide); (2) Phase deliverables with supporting data and statistical evidence; (3) 'Before and after' comparisons (control charts, capability histograms); (4) Financial impact validated by finance; (5) Next steps and resource requests. The presentation is data-dense and visual — heavy use of charts, statistical output, and process maps. The audience is typically the Champion, process owner, and other stakeholders. Standard templates exist but vary by organization. The format is deliberative — the presenter seeks approval, not just information sharing. For compressed communication, the project charter and control plan are the key documents.
traceability: Strong and formal: (1) Every decision is documented and linked to its evidence — root cause conclusions are traceable to specific hypothesis tests and data sets; (2) The Y = f(X) transfer function creates a formal, traceable link from process outputs (Y) to inputs (X); (3) All statistical analyses are archived with raw data, enabling independent re-analysis; (4) Tollgate review presentations serve as decision records — you can trace why a gate was passed or failed; (5) The project closure report provides a complete audit trail from problem statement through verified improvement to sustained control; (6) Financial benefits are independently validated by finance, creating an auditable financial trail; (7) Lessons learned are captured in the organizational knowledge base; (8) ISO 9001 and other quality standards often require this level of traceability, and DMAIC projects inherently satisfy those requirements. Rationale is preserved at every step — you can trace from outcome (reduced defect rate) back through the solution to the root cause analysis to the initial VOC data.
agent_communication_protocol: Not applicable — DMAIC is a human-executed methodology with no built-in agent communication protocol. In an LLM-agent adaptation: (1) Inter-agent communication would require standardized data exchange formats for SIPOC diagrams, process maps, FMEA tables, control charts, and statistical test results; (2) MCP or A2A could be adapted with custom tool definitions for each DMAIC phase deliverable; (3) Discovery mechanism would require agents to negotiate process ownership boundaries — which agent is responsible for which Y = f(X) analysis; (4) Tollgate reviews could be implemented as structured inter-agent review protocols with explicit pass/fail criteria; (5) Sync communication (request-response) would be needed for tollgate approvals; async for ongoing control monitoring. Custom protocol design would be required — no existing standard maps cleanly to DMAIC's structured phase-gate communication pattern.
learning_memory_architecture: Has a formal organizational learning architecture: (1) Semantic memory — the Body of Knowledge (BoK) codifies tools, techniques, and best practices; Belt certification preserves competency standards; (2) Episodic memory — each DMAIC project produces a project closure report archived in the lessons learned database; (3) Procedural memory — the DMAIC methodology itself is procedural knowledge, transferred through Belt training (classroom + coached project); (4) Post-hoc improvement — aggregate lessons learned are reviewed to improve organizational Six Sigma deployment, tool selection guidance, and training curricula; (5) The skill library is formal — Green Belt, Black Belt, and Master Black Belt certification tiers create a structured, transferable skill taxonomy; (6) For LLM adaptation: the project closure report database is an ideal resource for few-shot learning and fine-tuning; the tool library (FMEA, DOE, SPC, etc.) maps to function-calling capabilities; (7) No built-in distillation mechanism — the methodology relies on human practitioners to internalize lessons; automated distillation would require explicit extraction from project archives.
latency_bounds: Batch — typical DMAIC project duration is 3-6 months for Black Belt projects, 1-3 months for Green Belt projects. The methodology is intentionally slow and thorough — 'measure twice, cut once.' Real-time requirements exist only in the Control phase where SPC charts may require near-real-time monitoring (minutes to hours) for high-velocity manufacturing processes. Under time pressure: the methodology degrades significantly — rushing through Measure and Analyze produces unverified root causes and ineffective solutions (the most common criticism of 'fast Six Sigma' deployments). The minimum viable project duration is approximately 4 weeks for a focused Green Belt project with readily available data. Hard real-time: not applicable. Interactive: tollgate reviews are interactive presentations (~1-2 hours each). The methodology cannot be accelerated without compromising its core premise (data-driven decisions require data, which takes time to collect).
evaluation_benchmarking: No academic AI/LLM benchmarks apply. Evaluation is organizational and financial: (1) Financial impact — hard savings validated by finance (the primary metric); (2) Defect rate reduction — DPMO before vs. after; (3) Process capability improvement — Cpk before vs. after; (4) Project closure rate — percentage of chartered projects reaching Control phase; (5) Time-to-completion — average project duration; (6) Sustained benefits — Control phase metrics 12+ months post-closure; (7) Customer satisfaction impact — VOC metrics before vs. after; (8) ROI — program-level return on Six Sigma investment. Motorola reported $16B+ cumulative savings; GE reported $12B+ over 5 years. These organizational metrics have no equivalent in automated benchmarks like SWE-bench or GAIA. For LLM-based DMAIC automation, new benchmarks would need to measure statistical reasoning accuracy, root cause identification (against known ground truth), and improvement recommendation quality.
mapping_strength: Strong mapping to structured LLM agent workflows. What transfers directly: (1) The phase-gate structure maps perfectly to agent pipelines — each phase is a discrete reasoning step with explicit inputs and outputs; (2) Statistical analysis tools (hypothesis testing, regression, control charts) can be implemented as tool calls; (3) The structured artifact templates (charter, SIPOC, FMEA) are ideal for structured LLM output; (4) Tollgate reviews map to verification/validation steps in agent pipelines; (5) The Y = f(X) framework maps to causal reasoning. What needs translation: (1) DMAIC assumes a stable, measurable process baseline — software development processes are often less stable and harder to measure; (2) The 'defect' concept needs translation from manufacturing defects to software bugs, incidents, or failed requirements; (3) The methodology assumes experienced human judgment for FMEA scoring — LLMs can estimate but cannot ground truth these ratings without domain data; (4) The organizational roles (Champion, Belt, Process Owner) need mapping to agent roles or human-in-the-loop checkpoints.
gaps: What is missing for software/LLM agent contexts: (1) No mechanism for handling unmeasurable or rapidly changing processes — software development velocity and quality metrics are notoriously difficult to baseline; (2) The tollgate process assumes organizational authority structures that don't exist in autonomous agent systems — who is the 'Champion' for an LLM agent?; (3) DMAIC is designed for existing process improvement, not greenfield problem-solving — it cannot help if there is no process to measure yet; (4) The methodology treats variation as universally undesirable, but in software, variation can be innovation — some problems require divergent thinking, not variance reduction; (5) No built-in mechanism for handling novel problem types — the tool set assumes known failure modes; (6) The 3-6 month cycle time is incompatible with rapid software iteration; (7) DMAIC assumes human practitioners with domain expertise — LLMs may lack the tacit knowledge to identify which CTQs matter or what constitutes a 'defect' from a user's perspective.
adaptation_required: To apply DMAIC to the JORDAN pipeline: (1) Add a 'Measure Baseline' step before generating solutions — the agent must quantify the gap between current state and target state before proposing fixes; this directly addresses the JORDAN gap where solutions are generated without quantifying the problem; (2) Implement tollgate-like verification steps between pipeline phases — the agent should not proceed from research to planning without verifying that the problem is correctly defined and scoped; (3) Add a 'Statistical Verification' requirement to the root cause analysis step — root causes should be evidenced, not just hypothesized; (4) Add a 'Control Plan' equivalent to the synthesize/execute phases — how will the agent verify that its solution actually fixed the problem?; (5) Incorporate FMEA into the risk assessment node — systematically evaluate failure modes of proposed solutions before execution; (6) The MEASURE phase is the key missing piece in JORDAN — the agent should collect and analyze metrics on the problem before attempting to solve it. Recommended: add a MEASURE node between RESEARCH and PLAN that quantifies the problem baseline.
validation_context: Extensively validated in global manufacturing (Motorola: $16B+ savings; GE: $12B+ over 5 years; Honeywell, AlliedSignal, 3M, Ford, Samsung); transaction/service processes (Bank of America, Amazon, GE Capital); healthcare (Mayo Clinic, Mount Sinai, NHS — reducing medical errors, wait times, and infection rates); IT and software (reducing defect rates, improving deployment reliability); government (US Army, NASA, various federal agencies); aerospace and defense (rigorous statistical process control for safety-critical components). Formal academic validation through the ASQ Six Sigma Body of Knowledge and university programs. Most validation is ROI-based and organizational rather than controlled experimental. Criticism exists regarding the methodology's applicability to creative/knowledge work and whether the claimed savings are causally attributable to Six Sigma or to other concurrent initiatives.
known_failures: Documented failure modes: (1) Tool obsession — practitioners apply tools ritualistically without understanding the underlying problem; the project produces impressive-looking charts but no real improvement; (2) Wrong project selection — Six Sigma applied to problems that don't need statistical rigor (simple fixes) or that can't be measured (cultural issues); (3) Tollgate bureaucracy — the review process becomes more important than the improvement; projects take 12+ months and lose momentum; (4) Measurement system failure — bad data leads to wrong conclusions; the team 'improves' the measurement system rather than the process; (5) Statistical cherry-picking — practitioners select data and tests that confirm their preferred solution; (6) Control phase neglect — the project achieves improvement but the gains erode within 6-12 months because controls are not sustained after the Belt moves on; (7) Financial benefit inflation — teams claim savings that don't materialize in the P&L; (8) Resistance and cynicism — employees see Six Sigma as a box-ticking exercise or a threat to their autonomy; (9) Misapplication to creative work — trying to reduce 'variation' in activities where variation is valuable (innovation, design, customer relationships); (10) The 3M controversy — critics argue that Six Sigma stifled innovation at 3M by penalizing the experimentation and failure inherent to R&D; (11) Known counterexample: the 2008 financial crisis showed that even Six Sigma-certified financial processes could fail catastrophically when tail risks were excluded from measurement.
where_still_used: Manufacturing: automotive (Toyota, Ford, GM), electronics (Samsung, Intel), aerospace (Boeing, GE Aviation); Healthcare: Virginia Mason, Mayo Clinic, ThedaCare, NHS Trusts; Financial services: JP Morgan Chase, Bank of America, Citigroup; Technology: Amazon (operational excellence), Microsoft (business process improvement); Government: US Department of Defense, NASA, FDA; Consulting: major firms (McKinsey, BCG, Deloitte) have Six Sigma practices; Certification bodies: ASQ (American Society for Quality), IASSC (International Association for Six Sigma Certification), university programs (Villanova, Purdue, Arizona State); Active evolution: increasingly integrated with Agile (Agile Six Sigma), Lean (Lean Six Sigma), and data science (Six Sigma + big data analytics). The methodology remains a standard requirement in many manufacturing and operations management job descriptions.
